quick_facts.label: Award and level
quick_facts.value: B.Tech in Food Technology is a four-year undergraduate engineering programme focused on applying science and engineering to food processing, preservation, packaging, safety and quality systems.

quick_facts.label: School-level eligibility
quick_facts.value: Eligibility varies by institution. For the ICAR CUET (ICAR-UG) 2025 route, applicants needed Class 12 with at least three core subjects among Physics, Chemistry, Mathematics, Biology and Agriculture/Inter-Agriculture; individual universities may impose additional subject or score requirements.

quick_facts.label: Admissions landscape
quick_facts.value: Admissions are institution-specific and may use CUET (ICAR-UG), JEE Main, state engineering entrance tests, or university selection processes. JEE Main’s qualifying-subject rule for B.E./B.Tech at participating NITs, IIITs and CFTIs requires Physics and Mathematics plus one of Chemistry, Biotechnology, Biology or a technical-vocational subject.

quick_facts.label: Study themes
quick_facts.value: Representative curricula combine food chemistry, microbiology, processing and preservation, food engineering, packaging, product development, food safety, quality control and regulatory standards, with practical/laboratory and industry-learning elements.

quick_facts.label: Career relevance
quick_facts.value: A B.Tech in Food Technology can meet the essential-qualification requirement for some food-sector research project roles; for example, CSIR-CFTRI’s 2026 Project Associate-I notice accepts B.Tech Food Technology, among related degrees.

sections.heading: Eligibility: check the route and the receiving university
paragraphs: There is no single nationwide Class 12 eligibility rule for every B.Tech Food Technology seat. Under ICAR’s CUET (ICAR-UG) 2025 information bulletin, B.Tech Food Technology was among the bachelor’s programmes covered and candidates had to pass Class 12 with at least three core subjects selected from Physics, Chemistry, Mathematics, Biology and Agriculture/Inter-Agriculture. The bulletin also states that candidates must take CUET subjects consistent with the subjects passed in Class 12.

For a university-level illustration, Professor Jayashankar Telangana Agricultural University lists both Biology-stream (Bi.P.C.) and Mathematics-stream (M.P.C.) Class 12 candidates for its B.Tech Food Technology programme, with selection through TG-EAPCET and an ICAR-CUET route. Applicants should therefore verify the current prospectus of every target university, particularly its accepted subject combination, minimum marks, domicile/reservation rules and counselling conditions.
bullets: For ICAR-CUET-linked admission, match the CUET subjects to the Class 12 subjects accepted by the target programme.

For JEE Main/JoSAA- or CSAB-linked engineering admissions, the national qualifying-subject baseline is Physics and Mathematics plus one permitted third subject; institutions can have additional rules.
Some institutions permit diploma or B.Sc.-based lateral entry, but the qualifying discipline, minimum marks and availability of seats are institution-specific.

sections.heading: Duration and learning format
paragraphs: The conventional B.Tech Food Technology format is a four-year, eight-semester engineering degree after Class 12. An ICAR-aligned revised syllabus describes four years/eight semesters and 185 credits, allocating later-semester credit to experiential learning and in-plant training. Programme delivery commonly combines lectures with laboratory work, industrial exposure, internships and project work.

Exact credit totals, internship timing, elective baskets and the title of practical components differ between universities. A candidate should treat an individual university’s approved scheme of study—not a generic course listing—as the binding academic plan.
bullets: Typical entry point: first year after Class 12.
Typical exit award: B.Tech in Food Technology.
Applied components may include laboratory practicals, experiential learning, in-plant training, internships and a project.

sections.heading: What students study
paragraphs: The degree sits at the intersection of food science and engineering. Representative programme descriptions identify chemistry, microbiology, biochemistry, nutrition and engineering foundations, then apply them to processing, preservation, packaging, quality control, food safety, product development and regulatory compliance.

The ICAR-aligned curriculum example also embeds experiential learning and in-plant training, indicating that the degree is intended to develop production- and industry-facing capability as well as academic knowledge. Course names and the depth of specialisation—such as nutraceuticals, functional foods, biotechnology or sustainable production—will vary by institution.
bullets: Food processing and preservation operations.
Food chemistry, microbiology and biochemistry.
Food engineering and process design.
Packaging, storage, quality assurance and food-safety systems.
Product development, practical laboratory work and industry exposure.

sections.heading: Admission routes: ICAR, engineering tests and university processes
paragraphs: For the ICAR agricultural-university pathway, the 2025 admission bulletin used CUET (ICAR-UG) for bachelor’s programmes in agriculture and allied sciences, explicitly including B.Tech Food Technology. It directs candidates to check special conditions with the university of interest before applying, so programme availability and seat rules must be confirmed university by university.

Other institutions can use JEE Main, state-level engineering entrance examinations or their own process. PJTSAU, for example, publishes TG-EAPCET-based selection for B.Tech Food Technology alongside an ICAR-CUET route. NTA states that JEE Main is used for admissions to participating NITs, IIITs and CFTIs, but Food Technology availability must be checked in the current counselling seat matrix rather than assumed from the exam alone.

Entrance calendars, participating institutions, seat matrices, cut-offs and counselling rules change by admission year. The 2025 ICAR bulletin is useful route context, not a substitute for the current-year notice.
bullets: Identify whether the target college is an ICAR agricultural university, a state engineering/technical university, or a private/deemed university.
Read the current programme prospectus before selecting entrance-test subjects.
Confirm that the exact branch name—B.Tech Food Technology or a related Food Processing/Food Engineering title—is offered in the current intake.

sections.heading: Costs: highly institution-specific
paragraphs: Tuition is not standardized nationally. As one published institutional example, Janardan Rai Nagar Rajasthan Vidyapeeth University lists B.Tech Food Technology at ₹25,000 per semester; this is an example of that university’s published fee presentation, not a national benchmark or a prediction of the fee at another college.

Applicants should obtain the current official fee notice for each target institute and budget separately for tuition, examination charges, hostel and mess, laboratory/material charges, insurance, transport and any one-time deposits. Scholarships, fee waivers and state support can materially change net cost and should be assessed under the current admission-year rules.
bullets: Use only the target institution’s current fee notice for final budgeting.
Ask whether the stated fee excludes hostel, mess, examination, laboratory, caution-deposit or other mandatory charges.
Treat all fee figures as time-bound and subject to revision by the institution or government.

sections.heading: After graduation: postgraduate study, jobs and entrepreneurship
paragraphs: For postgraduate engineering study, M.Tech Food Technology is a direct progression option. Banaras Hindu University’s 2025 M.Tech Food Technology (Processing Technology) eligibility includes four-year B.Tech/B.E. degrees in Food Technology and several closely related food, dairy, agricultural and post-harvest engineering fields. Admission requirements, entrance tests and minimum grades remain programme-specific.

GATE is a relevant examination pathway for postgraduate and other opportunities. For GATE 2026, the official eligibility criteria permit B.E./B.Tech candidates who are in third year or higher, or who have completed the degree, to appear; the receiving institution determines its own admission rules and paper requirements.

Direct-entry roles may include food technologist, quality/quality-control analyst, graduate engineer trainee or research-assistant/project roles, depending on the employer and job specification. CSIR-CFTRI’s 2026 Project Associate-I notice is a concrete example of a research role that lists B.Tech Food Technology as an accepted qualification.

Entrepreneurship is another route. The Ministry of Food Processing Industries’ PMFME materials describe training in entrepreneurship, operations, marketing, bookkeeping, registration, hygiene, FSSAI standards and product-specific processing, packaging and storage. Scheme eligibility and assistance terms should be checked in the current official guidelines before making a business or financing decision.
bullets: M.Tech in Food Technology or related food-processing, food-engineering, dairy or post-harvest specialisations.
GATE, subject to the current brochure and the target institution’s admissions policy.
Industry roles in processing, operations, quality assurance/control, food safety, packaging and product development.
Research-project roles where the employer’s essential-qualification notice accepts the degree.
Food-processing enterprise development, with current PMFME or state-level support checked before application.
